FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, data release 5.0, 21 sites, children initially aged 9–10 years; methods state “The behavioral data were obtained from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (https://abcdstudy.org/; data release 5.0)”

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed-effects models and cross-lagged panel models over 4 annual waves; not a review or commentary

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Pediatrics Open Science 2025/2026; no indication of prior preprint or duplicate in corpus

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate β coefficients for “social media use × time” predicting inattention symptoms (β=0.03, p<.001); Figure 3 cross-lagged panel model reports social media use as IV predicting inattention (β=0.03, p=.004). Social media use is constructed from YSTS items 4 (texting) + 5 (visiting social networking sites) + 6 (video chat), a qualifying SM/phone-specific composite. Inattention symptoms (CBCL DSM-5 subscale) is an individual-trait-level outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized β coefficients with SEs and p-values reported throughout Table 2; cumulative effect size β=0.15 (SE=0.03, p<.001) in Figure 2B; cross-lagged β coefficients in Figure 3

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported: longitudinal β=0.03 (SE=0.01, p<.001) for social media × time on inattention; cumulative β=0.15 (SE=0.03, p<.001); cross-lagged β=0.03 (SE=0.01, p=.004)

FT_EC7_reason: SM is used as IV in cross-lagged panel models (social media at wave t predicting inattention at wave t+1, Figure 3) and in longitudinal interaction models (social media × time predicting symptom change, Table 2). EC7 does not apply when SM appears as IV in any analysis.
notes: Social media composite (texting + social networking + video chat) analyzed separately from TV/videos and gaming throughout. Cross-lagged models confirm SM-as-IV direction. PGS-ADHD included as moderator.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, ABCD 5.1 data release, 21 US sites, children aged 9–10 at baseline; methods state “Data for this study came from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, which recruited a diverse sample of children aged 9 to 10 years from 21 US sites”

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression and latent class trajectory modeling across three waves (Waves 1–3 of ABCD 4-year follow-up); not a review or commentary

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Obesity (Silver Spring) 2025; no indication of prior preprint or duplicate in corpus

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 2 and 3 report PR and RR for SMA (social media addiction scale adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale) predicting binge eating (BE) symptoms: Wave 1 addiction group PR=2.17 (95% CI: 1.22–3.84) in Table 2; longitudinal RR=1.90 (95% CI: 1.10–3.27) in Table 3. SMA is a qualifying problematic/addictive SM scale (Bergen-derived). Time spent on social media (SMT) also has its own PR/RR coefficients throughout. Binge eating symptoms (KSADS-5) is an individual-trait-level health outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: PR with 95% CI reported in Table 2 (cross-sectional); RR with 95% CI in Table 3 (prospective); trajectory-based RR in Table 4

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates: cross-sectional PR=2.17 (95% CI: 1.22–3.84) for SMA addiction group at Wave 1; prospective RR=1.90 (95% CI: 1.10–3.27); trajectory RR=3.01 (95% CI: 2.07–4.37) for consistently high SMA group

FT_EC7_reason: SM (SMA score and SMT) serves as IV throughout the prospective analyses (Wave 1 SMA/SMT predicting Wave 3 BE in Table 3; trajectory groups predicting Wave 3 BE in Table 4). EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV in the longitudinal models. Additionally, cross-sectional analyses at Wave 1 and Wave 3 have SM as IV predicting BE as DV.
notes: SMA (Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale adapted) and SMT analyzed as separate predictors. Moderator analyses by sex and BMI. Main analysis restricted to participants with a social media account; sensitivity analyses extend to the full sample.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, data release 5.0, 21 sites, children initially aged 9–10 years; methods state “We obtained data on brain structure, behavior, and demographic background from the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) Data Archive ABCD Data Release 5.0”

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and mediation analysis with cross-sectional and longitudinal data from ABCD; not a review or commentary

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Translational Psychiatry 2025; no indication of prior preprint or duplicate in corpus

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No separate phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. All results (Table S2, Table S3, Figure 1–4) use a single aggregated “screen time” variable. Step 2 composite check: Methods explicitly state screen time was “calculated as the total time spent using various devices, including playing video games and watching television.” This composite bundles TV/videos and video games with no phone/SM-specific breakout anywhere in main text or supplementary. Multi-activity aggregate including TV and gaming without separate phone/SM estimate → NOT_MET under Step 2.

notes: Screen time measure in ABCD YSTS covers TV, video games, and other devices and is reported as a single total. No breakout for phone/SM time in any table or supplement visible in the paper. Fails FT-IC2 Step 2 because composite includes TV and gaming without separation.
